persons.arguments_against: Smith is the commonest surname in the corpus (612 officials, 265 biographies), so the prior on any name-based match is weak, and one could posit a different P. J. C. Smith in Bermuda. This fails: Solicitor-General of Bermuda is a unique, named law office held by one person at a time; the biography prints 'solr.-gen., Berm., 1952' — exact office, exact colony, appointed the year before the stint begins; all three initials (P, J, C) agree; and bio and stint co-occur in exactly the same two editions (1953, 1954), appearing and disappearing together — the synchronised window itself indicates one person. Two distinct P. J. C. Smiths holding the same unique Bermuda law office in the same two years is not a realistic hypothesis. The honour FAIL is an artifact: neither source prints honours.
persons.would_overturn: Evidence that a different P. J. C. Smith held the Bermuda solicitor-generalship in 1953-1954, or that the biography subject never took up the 1952 appointment.

notes: Despite the maximally common surname, the match rests on a unique senior office (Solicitor-General, Bermuda) printed verbatim in the biography with consistent dates, full three-initial agreement, and perfectly synchronised edition windows (both appear only in 1953-1954).
